questions.specification: An architect is designing a right pyramid with a square base for a new monument. The base has a side length of 10 cm, and the height from the base to the apex is 12 cm.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Calculating the volume of specific 3D shapes given the parameters
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (b) involve calculating base area and the volume of a right square pyramid, which is a standard 3D volume application.

parts.content: Calculate the area of the square base.
parts.markscheme: - Calculate area: $\text{Area} = 10^2 = 100 \text{ cm}^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the base dimensions
steps.content:
To find the area of the base, we first need to identify its shape and dimensions from the question. We are told the monument is a right pyramid with a **square base**.

The side length of this square base is given as $10 \text{ cm}$.

steps.title: Use square area formula
steps.content:
The area of a square is calculated by multiplying the side length by itself, which is the same as squaring the side length. 

$$\text{Area} = \text{side}^2$$

steps.title: Calculate the area
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the given side length of $10 \text{ cm}$ into our formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Area} &= 10^2 \\ \text{Area} &= 100 \text{ cm}^2 \end{aligned}$$

This $100 \text{ cm}^2$ represents the value '$A$' (base area) that you would use if you were later asked to find the total volume of the pyramid using the formula from your data booklet.

gdc.instructions: Type 10^2 and press enter.

ti84.instructions: Enter 10 and press the [x²] key, then press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Enter 10 squared into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the square of the side length to find the area.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The area of the square base is $100 \text{ cm}^2$. 

In an IB exam, remember to always include the units ($\text{cm}^2$) if they are not provided in the answer line to ensure you get the full mark!

parts.content: Using your answer from part (a), determine the volume of the pyramid.
parts.markscheme:
- Using formula $V = \frac{1}{3} \times \text{Base Area} \times \text{Height}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substituting values and calculating: $V = \frac{1}{3} \times 100 \times 12 = 400 \text{ cm}^3$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the given dimensions
steps.content: To find the volume, we first need to extract the dimensions of the pyramid from the problem description. We are told the base is a square and given the vertical height.

highlights.text: height from the base to the apex is 12 cm

steps.title: Calculate the base area
steps.content:
The volume formula requires the area of the base ($A$). Since the base is a square, we multiply the side length by itself:

$$A = 10 \times 10 = 100 \text{ cm}^2$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 10 squared and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 10 squared and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 10 squared.

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the square base.

steps.title: Apply the volume formula
steps.content:
Now we use the standard formula for the volume of a pyramid found in the **Geometry and Trigonometry** section of your data booklet:

$$V = \frac{1}{3}Ah$$

Where:
- $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2$ (the area we just found)
- $h = 12 \text{ cm}$ (the height given in the question)

steps.title: Final volume calculation
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the formula to find the final result:

$$\begin{aligned} V &= \frac{1}{3} \times 100 \times 12 \\ V &= 400 \text{ cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

In the IB markscheme, you would earn one mark for showing the correct substitution into the formula and one mark for the correct final answer with units.

gdc.instructions: Enter (1/3) * 100 * 12 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1/3) * 100 * 12 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the final volume using the pyramid formula.

parts.content: Calculate the slant height of the pyramid, which is the distance from the midpoint of a base edge to the apex.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognizes need to use Pythagorean theorem with $\frac{s}{2}$ and height <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct substitution and calculation: $l = \sqrt{(\frac{10}{2})^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Visualize the right-angled triangle
steps.content:
To find the slant height, we need to look at a right-angled triangle inside the pyramid. This triangle is formed by:
1. The **vertical height** of the pyramid (from the center of the base to the apex).
2. The **horizontal distance** from the center of the base to the midpoint of one edge.
3. The **slant height**, which acts as the hypotenuse.

highlights.text: height from the base to the apex is 12 cm

steps.title: Calculate the base leg
steps.content:
The horizontal leg of our triangle is the distance from the center of the square base to the midpoint of an edge. Since the total side length is $10\text{ cm}$, this distance is exactly half of the side length.

$$\text{Base leg} = \frac{10}{2} = 5\text{ cm}$$

highlights.text: Recognizes need to use Pythagorean theorem with \frac{s}{2} and height

steps.title: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem
steps.content:
Now we use the Pythagorean Theorem, $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the legs (height and half-base) and $c$ is the slant height ($l$).

Substituting our known values:
$$5^2 + 12^2 = l^2$$

steps.title: Solve for slant height
steps.content:
We calculate the squares and then find the square root to determine the slant height $l$:

$$\begin{aligned} l^2 &= 25 + 144 \\ l^2 &= 169 \\ l &= \sqrt{169} \\ l &= 13\text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the calculation menu.
2. Enter sqrt(5^2 + 12^2).
3. Press [EXE] or [ENTER].

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] then [x²] (for the square root symbol).
2. Type 5^2 + 12^2.
3. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(5^2 + 12^2) into the input line.

calculator.description: Calculate the slant height using the Pythagorean theorem.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The slant height of the pyramid is $13\text{ cm}$. 

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for identifying the correct components for the Pythagorean Theorem and **1 mark** for the correct final answer.

steps.title: Visualize the right triangle
steps.content:
To find the slant height, we look at the right-angled triangle formed inside the pyramid. This triangle consists of:
1. The vertical **height** of the pyramid (12 cm).
2. The distance from the center of the base to the **midpoint of a base edge**.
3. The **slant height**, which is the hypotenuse of this triangle.

This falls under **Topic 3.2: 2D and 3D trigonometry**.

highlights.text: height from the base to the apex is 12 cm

steps.title: Calculate the horizontal distance
steps.content:
The distance from the center of a square base to the midpoint of one of its edges is half the total side length of the base.

$$d = \frac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Find the angle of elevation
steps.content:
Using the tangent ratio $(\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}})$, we can find the angle of elevation $(\theta)$ from the midpoint of the base to the apex:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{12}{5}$$

$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{12}{5}\right) \approx 67.38^\circ$$

gdc.instructions: Use the atan or tan⁻¹ function with the value 12/5.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [2nd] [TAN] (12 / 5) [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Switch to degree mode in settings, then type: arctan(12/5).

calculator.description: Calculate the angle of elevation using the inverse tangent function.

steps.title: Calculate the slant height
steps.content:
Now we use a trigonometric ratio to find the hypotenuse (the slant height, $l$). Using the sine ratio $(\sin \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}})$:

$$\sin(67.38^\circ) = \frac{12}{l}$$

Rearranging for $l$:

$$l = \frac{12}{\sin(67.38^\circ)} = 13 \text{ cm}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 12 by the sine of the angle stored in your calculator.

ti84.instructions: Press 12 / SIN(Ans) [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the slant height using the sine ratio with the previously found angle.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The slant height of the pyramid is **13 cm**.

Note: Even though we used trigonometry, the result matches the Pythagorean calculation $(\sqrt{5^2 + 12^2} = 13)$ required by the markscheme.

parts.content: Find the total surface area of the pyramid, including the base and all triangular faces.
parts.markscheme:
- Total surface area = Area of base + 4 $\times$ Area of one triangular face

- $= 100 + 4 \times (\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 13)$

- $= 100 + 260$

- $= 360 \text{ cm}^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Establish the ground line
steps.content:
To begin our 2D geometric construction, we represent the ground as a horizontal baseline. Mark two points on this line to represent observer $A$ and observer $B$. Since the question states they are $100$ meters apart, we label the segment $AB$ with this distance.

This setup follows **Topic 3: Geometry and trigonometry**, specifically focusing on 2D representations of 3D scenarios.

highlights.text: observers, A and B, who are standing 100 meters apart

steps.title: Position the balloon
steps.content:
Next, place a point $P$ above the line segment $AB$ to represent the hot air balloon. The problem specifies the balloon is "directly above a point on the line segment," meaning it sits somewhere between $A$ and $B$ horizontally.

Draw a vertical dashed line from the balloon down to the baseline to represent its height ($h$). This creates two right-angled triangles sharing a common vertical side.

highlights.text: directly above a point on the ground that lies on the line segment connecting two observers, A and B

steps.title: Label angles of elevation
steps.content:
Now, connect observer $A$ to the balloon and observer $B$ to the balloon. The **angle of elevation** is the angle measured upwards from the horizontal ground line to the line of sight.

*   At point $A$, label the angle as $40^\circ$.
*   At point $B$, label the angle as $60^\circ$.

highlights.text: angle of elevation from observer A to the balloon is 40^∘
angle of elevation from observer B is 60^∘

steps.title: Finalize and check diagram
steps.content:
Review your diagram to ensure all elements required by the markscheme are present:

1.  **Positions:** Balloon ($P$), Observers ($A, B$).
2.  **Horizontal distance:** $100$ m marked between $A$ and $B$.
3.  **Angles:** $40^\circ$ and $60^\circ$ clearly marked at the correct observers.
4.  **Height:** Vertical line labeled $h$.

According to the markscheme, all these elements are necessary to secure both marks. Using dashed lines for the height and standard arc notation for angles helps keep the diagram professional and clear.

parts.content: Calculate the height of the balloon above the ground based on the observations from points A and B.
parts.markscheme:
Let height of balloon be $h$ and horizontal distance from A to the point directly below the balloon be $x$.
<marks>M1</marks>

From A: $h = x\tan(40^\circ)$
From B: $h = (100-x)\tan(60^\circ)$
<marks>A1</marks>

Equating and solving:
$$x\tan(40^\circ) = (100-x)\tan(60^\circ)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(\tan(40^\circ) + \tan(60^\circ)) &= 100\tan(60^\circ) \\
x &\approx 67.4 \text{ m} \\
h &\approx 56.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

<examTip>Draw a clear diagram showing the two triangles formed to help visualize the problem.</examTip>

[3 marks total]

- Calculate the angle between the normal and the direction vector (or observe they are parallel): $\cos \phi = \frac{\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{d}}{|\mathbf{n}| |\mathbf{d}|} = \frac{4 + 1 + 1}{\sqrt{6}\sqrt{6}} = 1 \implies \phi = 0^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>

questions.specification:
A cylindrical water tank with a radius of 5 cm and a height of 12 cm is topped with a cone of the same radius and a height of 9 cm.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0b4066da-3853-48c0-8cb3-baae8137377e)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry and Trigonometry > SL 3.1—3d space, volume, angles, midpoints > Question Type 6: Calculating the volume of specific complex shapes made by combining two or more shapes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 6: Calculating the volume of specific complex shapes made by combining two or more shapes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for the total volume of a composite 3D solid (cylinder topped with a cone).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry and Trigonometry > SL 3.1—3d space, volume, angles, midpoints > Question Type 5: Calculating the surface area of specific complex shapes made by combining two or more shapes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 5: Calculating the surface area of specific complex shapes made by combining two or more shapes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for the total surface area of a composite 3D solid, including curved surfaces and specified bases.

parts.content: Calculate the total volume of the combined shape, which represents the maximum capacity of the tank.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate volume of cylinder: $V_{cyl} = \pi r^2 h = \pi(5)^2(12) = 300\pi$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate volume of cone: $V_{cone} = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h = \frac{1}{3}\pi(5)^2(9) = 75\pi$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate total volume: $300\pi + 75\pi = 375\pi \text{ cm}^3$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify dimensions and components
steps.content:
The tank is a composite shape made of two parts: a **cylinder** at the bottom and a **cone** on top. From the question, we identify the following measurements:

*   **Radius ($r$):** Both shapes have a radius of $5\text{ cm}$.
*   **Cylinder height ($h_{cyl}$):** $12\text{ cm}$.
*   **Cone height ($h_{cone}$):** $9\text{ cm}$.

steps.title: Calculate cylinder volume
steps.content:
The volume of a cylinder is calculated by multiplying the area of the circular base by its height: 

$$V_{cyl} = \pi r^2 h$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} V_{cyl} &= \pi \times (5)^2 \times 12 \\ &= \pi \times 25 \times 12 \\ &= 300\pi \text{ cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cone volume
steps.content:
To find the volume of the cone, we use the formula for a pyramid from the data booklet, where the base area $A = \pi r^2$:

$$V = \frac{1}{3}Ah$$

Substituting our values ($r=5$, $h=9$):
$$\begin{aligned} V_{cone} &= \frac{1}{3} \times \pi \times (5)^2 \times 9 \\ &= \frac{1}{3} \times \pi \times 25 \times 9 \\ &= 75\pi \text{ cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine total volume
steps.content:
Finally, we add the two volumes together to find the total capacity of the tank:

$$\begin{aligned} V_{total} &= V_{cyl} + V_{cone} \\ &= 300\pi + 75\pi \\ &= 375\pi \text{ cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, this is approximately $1180 \text{ cm}^3$.

gdc.instructions: Type 375 * π into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 375 * π and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 375 * pi into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total volume using the exact expression.

steps.title: Identify the common factors
steps.content:
To calculate the total volume using a single expression, notice that both the cylinder and the cone share the same base radius of $r = 5$ cm. 

The total volume is the sum of the cylinder volume and the cone volume:
$$V_{total} = V_{cylinder} + V_{cone}$$

From our general knowledge and the data booklet, we know:
- Volume of a cylinder: $V = \pi r^2 h_{cyl}$
- Volume of a cone: $V = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h_{cone}$

steps.title: Create the combined formula
steps.content:
Since both parts share the term $\pi r^2$, we can factor it out to create one efficient formula for the total capacity:

$$V_{total} = \pi r^2 \left( h_{cyl} + \frac{1}{3}h_{cone} \right)$$

This method is great because it reduces the number of steps and helps avoid rounding errors midway through the calculation.

steps.title: Substitute the given values
steps.content:
Now, let's plug in the specific values from the question:
- Radius $r = 5$ cm
- Cylinder height $h_{cyl} = 12$ cm
- Cone height $h_{cone} = 9$ cm

$$\begin{aligned} V_{total} &= \pi (5)^2 \left( 12 + \frac{1}{3}(9) \right) \\ &= \pi (25) (12 + 3) \\ &= 25 \times 15 \times \pi \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final volume
steps.content:
Using the expression we built, we find the final volume:

$$V_{total} = 375\pi \text{ cm}^3$$

In an IB exam, you can leave your answer in terms of $\pi$ unless the question asks for a decimal. If you need a decimal, $375\pi \approx 1178.1$ cm$^3$ (to 1 decimal place).

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the combined formula into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total volume in one step using the factored formula.

parts.content: Determine the total surface area of the combined shape, including the base of the tank and the cone, to understand the material required for manufacturing.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate area of cylinder base: $A_{base} = \pi(5)^2 = 25\pi$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate lateral area of cylinder: $A_{cyl} = 2\pi(5)(12) = 120\pi$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate slant height of cone: $l = \sqrt{5^2 + 9^2} = \sqrt{106}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate lateral area of cone: $A_{cone} = \pi(5)\sqrt{106} = 5\pi\sqrt{106}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate total surface area: $25\pi + 120\pi + 5\pi\sqrt{106} = 145\pi + 5\pi\sqrt{106} \text{ cm}^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

<note>The top circular face of the cylinder is not included as it is where the cone is attached.</note>

steps.title: Analyze the combined surface
steps.content:
To find the total surface area, we need to decompose the shape into its external surfaces. The shape consists of:
1. The **circular base** at the very bottom.
2. The **lateral surface** (the curved side) of the cylinder.
3. The **lateral surface** (the curved side) of the cone.

Note that the circular top of the cylinder and the base of the cone are pressed together on the inside, so they are not part of the external surface area.

steps.title: Calculate the circular base
steps.content:
The base of the tank is a circle with a radius of $5$ cm. We use the area formula for a circle:

$$A_{base} = \pi r^2$$

Substituting $r = 5$:

$$A_{base} = \pi(5)^2 = 25\pi \text{ cm}^2$$

steps.title: Calculate cylindrical lateral area
steps.content:
Next, we find the area of the curved side of the cylinder. The formula for the lateral area of a cylinder is:

$$A_{cyl} = 2\pi rh$$

Using $r = 5$ cm and $h = 12$ cm:

$$A_{cyl} = 2\pi(5)(12) = 120\pi \text{ cm}^2$$

steps.title: Find cone's slant height
steps.content:
To find the lateral area of the cone, we first need its slant height ($l$). We can find this by looking at the right-angled triangle formed by the cone's radius ($r = 5$) and its vertical height ($h = 9$). Using the **Pythagorean theorem**:

$$l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$$

$$l = \sqrt{5^2 + 9^2}$$

$$l = \sqrt{25 + 81} = \sqrt{106} \text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Calculate conical lateral area
steps.content:
Now we use the slant height to find the lateral surface area of the cone using the formula:

$$A_{cone} = \pi rl$$

Substituting $r = 5$ and $l = \sqrt{106}$:

$$A_{cone} = \pi(5)(\sqrt{106}) = 5\pi\sqrt{106} \text{ cm}^2$$

steps.title: Sum the total area
steps.content:
Finally, we add all the external surface areas together:

$$\text{Total Area} = A_{base} + A_{cyl} + A_{cone}$$

$$\text{Total Area} = 25\pi + 120\pi + 5\pi\sqrt{106}$$

Combining the like terms ($25\pi$ and $120\pi$):

$$\text{Total Area} = (145\pi + 5\pi\sqrt{106}) \text{ cm}^2$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, type 145π + 5π√(106) and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter (145 * π) + (5 * π * √(106)) into the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the final expression into a new expression line to see the decimal result.

steps.title: Identify the components
steps.content:
To calculate the total surface area ($SA_{total}$) of the combined shape, we need to sum the surface areas of the individual exposed parts:
1.  **Cylinder Base**: A circle at the bottom.
2.  **Cylinder Lateral Area**: The curved vertical surface.
3.  **Cone Lateral Area**: The slanted surface on top.

*Note: The interface where the cylinder and cone meet is internal and not part of the surface area.*

highlights.text: total surface area of the combined shape, including the base of the tank and the cone

steps.title: Calculate cylinder surface areas
steps.content:
First, we calculate the area of the circular base and the curved lateral surface of the cylinder using the radius $r = 5$ cm and height $h = 12$ cm.

Using the formula for the area of a circle:
$$A_{base} = \pi r^2 = \pi (5)^2 = 25\pi \text{ cm}^2$$

Using the formula for the lateral area of a cylinder:
$$A_{cyl\_lat} = 2\pi rh = 2\pi (5)(12) = 120\pi \text{ cm}^2$$

steps.title: Determine cone's base angle
steps.content:
To find the lateral area of the cone, we need the slant height ($l$). Following the **Trigonometric Slant Height Method**, we first find the base angle $\theta$ of the cone. 

In the right-angled triangle formed by the cone's height ($h_{cone} = 9$) and radius ($r = 5$):
$$\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{9}{5}$$

Solving for $\theta$:
$$\theta = \arctan(1.8) \approx 60.945^\circ$$

gdc.instructions: Enter atan(9/5) in degree mode.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TAN] (9/5) [ENTER]. Ensure mode is set to DEGREES.

desmos.instructions: Type arctan(9/5) and ensure degree mode is selected.

calculator.description: Calculate the base angle theta using the arctan function.

steps.title: Calculate slant height
steps.content:
Now we use the cosine ratio to find the slant height ($l$), which is the hypotenuse of the triangle:
$$\cos \theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{r}{l}$$

Rearranging to solve for $l$:
$$l = \frac{5}{\cos(60.945...^\circ)} \approx 10.296 \text{ cm}$$

This value is exactly $\sqrt{5^2 + 9^2} = \sqrt{106}$, matching the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: Divide the radius by the cosine of the previously found angle.

ti84.instructions: Type 5 / cos(Ans) [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 5 / cos(60.945 degrees).

calculator.description: Calculate the slant height using the cosine of the stored angle.

steps.title: Calculate cone lateral area
steps.content:
Using the slant height $l = \sqrt{106}$ and radius $r = 5$, we apply the lateral surface area formula for a cone:
$$A_{cone\_lat} = \pi r l = \pi (5)(\sqrt{106}) = 5\pi\sqrt{106} \text{ cm}^2$$

steps.title: Calculate total surface area
steps.content:
Finally, we sum all components:
$$SA_{total} = A_{base} + A_{cyl\_lat} + A_{cone\_lat}$$
$$SA_{total} = 25\pi + 120\pi + 5\pi\sqrt{106}$$
$$SA_{total} = 145\pi + 5\pi\sqrt{106} \text{ cm}^2$$

Evaluating this for a numerical answer:
$$SA_{total} \approx 617 \text{ cm}^2$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the total sum of the three surface areas.

ti84.instructions: Type 145π + 5π√(106) [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Enter the final sum expression.

calculator.description: Sum the components to find the final surface area.

steps.title: Calculate the cylinder base area
steps.content:
The base of the water tank is a circle with a radius of $5\text{ cm}$. In our 2D net, this is a single circle.

Using the area of a circle formula:
$$A_{\text{base}} = \pi r^2$$

Substituting the given radius:
$$A_{\text{base}} = \pi(5)^2 = 25\pi \approx 78.539... \text{ cm}^2$$

steps.title: Calculate the cylinder wall area
steps.content:
When we "unroll" the lateral surface (wall) of the cylinder, it forms a rectangle in our 2D net. 
- The **height** of this rectangle is the height of the cylinder: $12\text{ cm}$.
- The **width** of this rectangle is the circumference of the circular base: $2\pi r$.

First, find the width (circumference):
$$C = 2\pi(5) = 10\pi$$

Now, calculate the area of this rectangular piece:
$$A_{\text{cyl}} = \text{width} \times \text{height} = 10\pi \times 12 = 120\pi \approx 376.991... \text{ cm}^2$$

steps.title: Determine the cone's slant height
steps.content:
To calculate the area of the cone's wall, we need the slant height ($l$). In the cone, the radius ($5\text{ cm}$), the height ($9\text{ cm}$), and the slant height form a right-angled triangle. We use the Pythagorean theorem:

$$\begin{aligned} l &= \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} \\ l &= \sqrt{5^2 + 9^2} \\ l &= \sqrt{25 + 81} \\ l &= \sqrt{106} \approx 10.295... \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the cone wall area
steps.content:
When the lateral surface of a cone is flattened, it forms a **circular sector** in our 2D net. 
- The **radius** of this sector is the slant height of the cone ($l = \sqrt{106}$).
- The **arc length** of this sector is the circumference of the cone's base ($10\pi$).

Using the relationship for sector area $A = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{arc length} \times \text{radius}$:
$$A_{\text{cone}} = \frac{1}{2} \times (10\pi) \times \sqrt{106} = 5\pi\sqrt{106} \approx 161.723... \text{ cm}^2$$

steps.title: Sum the total surface area
steps.content:
To find the total surface area, we sum the areas of the individual components of our 2D net. Note that the top circular face of the cylinder is excluded because the cone is attached there.

$$\text{Total Area} = A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{cyl}} + A_{\text{cone}}$$
$$\text{Total Area} = 25\pi + 120\pi + 5\pi\sqrt{106}$$
$$\text{Total Area} = 145\pi + 5\pi\sqrt{106} \approx 617.254... \text{ cm}^2$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculated terms and use the π constant for high precision.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen using the π button (2nd + ^) and the square root button (2nd + x²).

desmos.instructions: Type the exact expression into the calculator bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final surface area value to 3 significant figures.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The total surface area required for manufacturing is:
$$\text{Total Surface Area} = 145\pi + 5\pi\sqrt{106} \text{ cm}^2 \approx 617 \text{ cm}^2$$

parts.content: Hence or otherwise, find the two vectors of magnitude $45$ that are perpendicular to both $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$.
parts.markscheme:
METHOD 1
 - Attempt at vector product $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}$ <marks>M1</marks>
 - Calculate vector product: $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} (2)(2) - (-2)(1) \\ -((1)(2) - (-2)(2)) \\ (1)(1) - (2)(2) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ -6 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>
 - Find magnitude of $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}$: $\sqrt{6^2 + (-6)^2 + (-3)^2} = \sqrt{81} = 9$ <marks>A1</marks>
 - Scale to magnitude $45$: $\pm \frac{45}{9} \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ -6 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix} = \pm \begin{pmatrix} 30 \\ -30 \\ -15 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

METHOD 2
 - Let the required vector be $\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix}$.
 - Set up perpendicularity equations: $x + 2y - 2z = 0$ and $2x + y + 2z = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
 - Solve for $y$ and $z$ in terms of $x$: $y = -x, z = -\frac{1}{2}x$ (or equivalent) <marks>A1</marks>
 - Use magnitude condition: $x^2 + (-x)^2 + (-\frac{1}{2}x)^2 = 45^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
 - Solve for $x$: $\frac{9}{4}x^2 = 2025 \Rightarrow x^2 = 900 \Rightarrow x = \pm 30$
 - Final vectors: $\begin{pmatrix} 30 \\ -30 \\ -15 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\begin{pmatrix} -30 \\ 30 \\ 15 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

Award A1 for expression without $\mathbf{r} =$, e.g. $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$.

Award A1 for $L_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$.

Award A0 for $\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$.

parts.content: Calculate the lengths of each side of the triangular plot: AB, BC, and AC.
parts.markscheme:

- Calculate length AB: $AB = \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (5-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate lengths BC and AC: $BC = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{17}$ and $AC = \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (4-1)^2} = \sqrt{10}$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award full marks for correct answers with clear working shown</note>

parts.content: Determine whether triangle ABC is a right-angled triangle, explaining your reasoning and method.
parts.markscheme:
- State using Pythagorean theorem to check if sides satisfy $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute and calculate values: $AB^2 = 25$, $AC^2 = 10$, $BC^2 = 17$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Conclude that $AC^2 + BC^2 \neq AB^2$ ($10 + 17 \neq 25$) and triangle ABC is not right-angled <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

<examTip>Remember to clearly state which angle is hypothetically the right angle (opposite the longest side) in your conclusion</examTip>

steps.title: Define the strategy
steps.content:
To determine if triangle ABC is right-angled, we will calculate the squared lengths of all three sides. According to the **Pythagorean Theorem**, if a triangle is right-angled, the sum of the squares of the two shorter sides must equal the square of the longest side:

$$a^2 + b^2 = c^2$$

This is a fundamental technique covered in **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry**.

steps.title: Calculate side $AB^2$
steps.content:
We use the squared distance formula, which is derived from the distance formula found in the data booklet under **Topic 3.1**: 

$$d^2 = (x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2$$

For side $AB$ with points $A(2,1)$ and $B(5,5)$:

$$\begin{aligned} AB^2 &= (5 - 2)^2 + (5 - 1)^2 \\ AB^2 &= 3^2 + 4^2 \\ AB^2 &= 9 + 16 \\ AB^2 &= 25 \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns you part of the second mark (**A1**).

gdc.instructions: Type (5-2)^2 + (5-1)^2 into the calculation bar.

ti84.instructions: Enter (5-2)^2 + (5-1)^2 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (5-2)^2 + (5-1)^2 into an expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the squared distance for AB.

steps.title: Calculate side $AC^2$
steps.content:
Now we calculate the squared distance for side $AC$ using points $A(2,1)$ and $C(1,4)$:

$$\begin{aligned} AC^2 &= (1 - 2)^2 + (4 - 1)^2 \\ AC^2 &= (-1)^2 + 3^2 \\ AC^2 &= 1 + 9 \\ AC^2 &= 10 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type (1-2)^2 + (4-1)^2 into the calculation bar.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1-2)^2 + (4-1)^2 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (1-2)^2 + (4-1)^2 into an expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the squared distance for AC.

steps.title: Calculate side $BC^2$
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the squared distance for side $BC$ using points $B(5,5)$ and $C(1,4)$:

$$\begin{aligned} BC^2 &= (1 - 5)^2 + (4 - 5)^2 \\ BC^2 &= (-4)^2 + (-1)^2 \\ BC^2 &= 16 + 1 \\ BC^2 &= 17 \end{aligned}$$

Having all three values ($AB^2=25$, $AC^2=10$, $BC^2=17$) completes the **A1** mark requirement.

gdc.instructions: Type (1-5)^2 + (4-5)^2 into the calculation bar.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1-5)^2 + (4-5)^2 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (1-5)^2 + (4-5)^2 into an expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the squared distance for BC.

steps.title: Test the Pythagorean Theorem
steps.content:
Identify the longest side, which is $AB^2 = 25$. If the triangle is right-angled at $C$, then $AC^2 + BC^2$ should equal $AB^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} AC^2 + BC^2 &= 10 + 17 \\ AC^2 + BC^2 &= 27 \end{aligned}$$

Comparing this to the longest side:

$$27 \neq 25$$

This check is essential for the final reasoning mark (**R1**).

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since $AC^2 + BC^2 \neq AB^2$ ($10 + 17 \neq 25$), the lengths do not satisfy the Pythagorean theorem. 

Therefore, **triangle ABC is not a right-angled triangle**. Specifically, there is no right angle at vertex C (the angle opposite the longest side).

steps.title: Define the Gradient Method
steps.content:
To determine if triangle $ABC$ is right-angled, we can check if any two sides are perpendicular. Two lines are perpendicular if the product of their gradients (slopes) is $-1$. 

We will use the gradient formula from the data booklet:

$$m = \frac{y_{2}-y_{1}}{x_{2}-x_{1}}$$

steps.title: Calculate gradient of AB
steps.content:
Using the coordinates $A(2, 1)$ and $B(5, 5)$, we calculate the gradient $m_{AB}$:

$$\begin{aligned} m_{AB} &= \frac{5 - 1}{5 - 2} \\ m_{AB} &= \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.33 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (5 - 1) / (5 - 2).

ti84.instructions: Enter (5 - 1) / (5 - 2) on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient of side AB.

steps.title: Calculate gradient of BC
steps.content:
Using the coordinates $B(5, 5)$ and $C(1, 4)$, we calculate the gradient $m_{BC}$:

$$\begin{aligned} m_{BC} &= \frac{4 - 5}{1 - 5} \\ m_{BC} &= \frac{-1}{-4} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (4 - 5) / (1 - 5).

ti84.instructions: Enter (4 - 5) / (1 - 5) on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient of side BC.

steps.title: Calculate gradient of AC
steps.content:
Using the coordinates $A(2, 1)$ and $C(1, 4)$, we calculate the gradient $m_{AC}$:

$$\begin{aligned} m_{AC} &= \frac{4 - 1}{1 - 2} \\ m_{AC} &= \frac{3}{-1} = -3 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (4 - 1) / (1 - 2).

ti84.instructions: Enter (4 - 1) / (1 - 2) on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient of side AC.

steps.title: Check for perpendicular sides
steps.content:
We now test if the product of any two gradients equals $-1$:

1.  $m_{AB} \times m_{BC} = \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{3} \neq -1$
2.  $m_{BC} \times m_{AC} = \frac{1}{4} \times (-3) = -\frac{3}{4} \neq -1$
3.  $m_{AB} \times m_{AC} = \frac{4}{3} \times (-3) = -4 \neq -1$

Since no product equals $-1$, no two sides are perpendicular to each other.

steps.title: State final conclusion
steps.content:
Because none of the pairs of gradients are negative reciprocals of each other (their products are not $-1$), triangle $ABC$ does not contain a $90^{\circ}$ angle. 

Therefore, **triangle $ABC$ is not a right-angled triangle**.

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: To determine if triangle $ABC$ is right-angled using the **Cosine Rule**, we first need to find the lengths of all three sides: $AB$, $BC$, and $AC$. If the triangle has a $90^{\circ}$ angle, the Cosine Rule will reveal that the cosine of that angle is zero ($ \cos 90^{\circ} = 0 $).

steps.title: Calculate side lengths
steps.content:
We use the distance formula (derived from **Topic 3.1**) to find the lengths of the sides between points $A(2,1)$, $B(5,5)$, and $C(1,4)$:

$$\begin{aligned} AB &= \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (5-1)^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \\ BC &= \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{(-4)^2 + (-1)^2} = \sqrt{17} \\ AC &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (4-1)^2} = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{10} \end{aligned}$$

These values are consistent with the squares provided in the markscheme: $AB^2 = 25$, $BC^2 = 17$, and $AC^2 = 10$.

ti84.instructions: Enter (5-2)^2 + (5-1)^2 to find AB squared, then (1-5)^2 + (4-5)^2 for BC squared, and (1-2)^2 + (4-1)^2 for AC squared.

desmos.instructions: Input the squares of the distances based on the coordinates.

calculator.description: Calculate the lengths of the sides squared to verify coordinates.

steps.title: Select the largest angle
steps.content: A right angle, if it exists, must be opposite the longest side. Since $AB = 5$ is the longest side ($5 = \sqrt{25}$, which is greater than $\sqrt{17}$ and $\sqrt{10}$), the potential right angle is at vertex $C$.

steps.title: Apply the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
We use the **Cosine Rule** from the data booklet to find $\cos C$. 

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

Rearranging to solve for $\cos C$:

$$\cos C = \frac{a^2 + b^2 - c^2}{2ab}$$

Substituting our side lengths where $c=AB$, $a=BC$, and $b=AC$:

$$\cos C = \frac{(\sqrt{17})^2 + (\sqrt{10})^2 - 5^2}{2(\sqrt{17})(\sqrt{10})}$$

steps.title: Evaluate the result
steps.content:
Calculate the numerator and denominator to find the value of $\cos C$:

$$\cos C = \frac{17 + 10 - 25}{2\sqrt{170}} = \frac{2}{2\sqrt{170}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{170}}$$

Since $\cos C = \frac{1}{\sqrt{170}} \approx 0.0767$, and we know that for a right angle $\cos 90^{\circ} = 0$, angle $C$ is **not** $90^{\circ}$.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the cosine value.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the cosine of angle C.

steps.title: Formulate the conclusion
steps.content:
Because the largest angle (opposite the longest side $AB$) does not have a cosine of zero, it is not a $90^{\circ}$ angle. 

Conclusion: Triangle $ABC$ is **not** a right-angled triangle because $AC^2 + BC^2 \neq AB^2$ (which is effectively what $\cos C \neq 0$ shows).

highlights.text: Conclude that $AC^2 + BC^2 \neq AB^2$ ($10 + 17 \neq 25$) and triangle ABC is not right-angled

parts.content: Find the area of the field.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the area of the field using the area of a triangle formula: $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times 110 \times 85 \times \sin 55^\circ$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>
- State the area: $\text{Area} = 3830 \text{ m}^2$ ($3829.53\dots \text{ m}^2$) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Units must be given for the final **A1** to be awarded.</note>

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To find the area of the triangular field, we first identify the known side lengths and the angle between them (the included angle):

- Side $c = 85 \text{ m}$ (side $[AB]$)
- Side $b = 110 \text{ m}$ (side $[AC]$)
- Included angle $A = 55^\circ$

This information is perfect for the trigonometric area formula because we have two sides and the angle "sandwiched" between them.

highlights.text: angle between these two sides is 55°

steps.title: Select the area formula
steps.content:
From the **Geometry and Trigonometry** section of your IB Data Booklet, we find the formula for the area of a triangle when two sides and the included angle are known:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$$

In our case, we can adapt the letters to match our triangle: $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times AB \times AC \times \sin(A)$.

steps.title: Substitute the values
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the given values into the formula. This step usually earns the first method mark (**M1**) in an IB exam:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times 110 \times 85 \times \sin(55^\circ)$$ 

Before you calculate, make sure your calculator is set to **DEGREE** mode, as the angle is given in degrees.

gdc.instructions:
1. Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode.
2. Enter the expression: 0.5 × 110 × 85 × sin(55)
3. Execute the calculation.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and ensure 'DEGREE' is highlighted.
2. Press [2ND] [MODE] to return to the home screen.
3. Type: 0.5 * 110 * 85 * sin(55)
4. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Settings) and select 'Degrees'.
2. Type: 1/2 * 110 * 85 * sin(55)

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the triangle using the given sides and angle.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Evaluating the expression gives us:

$$\text{Area} \approx 3829.53...$$

Rounding to the nearest whole number or three significant figures as suggested by the markscheme, and including the correct units:

$$\text{Area} = 3830 \text{ m}^2$$

Don't forget the units ($ \text{m}^2 $)! In IB Math AI, the final accuracy mark often requires correct units for area problems.

steps.title: Visualize the geometry
steps.content:
To find the area of the triangular field, we can use the geometric approach of finding the perpendicular height. 

We are given:
- Side $[AB] = 85 \text{ m}$ (let's treat this as the hypotenuse of a smaller right-angled triangle)
- Side $[AC] = 110 \text{ m}$ (we'll use this as our base)
- Included angle $A = 55^\circ$

Our goal is to find the height $h$ dropped from vertex $B$ down to the base $AC$.

highlights.text: angle between these two sides is 55^\circ

steps.title: Calculate the perpendicular height
steps.content:
Using right-angled trigonometry (specifically the sine ratio), we can find the height $h$:

$$\sin(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$$

In our triangle:
$$\sin(55^\circ) = \frac{h}{85}$$

Rearranging for $h$:
$$h = 85 \times \sin(55^\circ)$$ 

This gives us a height of approximately $69.628 \text{ m}$.

gdc.instructions: Set angle units to degrees. Calculate 85 * sin(55).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [8][5] [SIN] [5][5] [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Set mode to Degrees. Type: 85 * sin(55).

calculator.description: Calculate the height using the sine function.

steps.title: Calculate the triangle area
steps.content:
Now we use the general formula for the area of a triangle:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

Substituting our values ($base = 110$ and $height = 85 \sin 55^\circ$):

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times 110 \times (85 \sin 55^\circ)$$

This calculation combines the base measurement with the height we just derived.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.5 * 110 * 85 * sin(55).

calculator.description: Calculate the final area of the field.

steps.title: Final answer and units
steps.content:
Performing the calculation:

$$\text{Area} \approx 3829.535...$$

Rounding to the nearest whole number (or 3 significant figures as per standard IB practice) and including the correct units:

$$\text{Area} = 3830 \text{ m}^2$$

parts.content:
The farmer would like to divide the field into two equal parts by constructing a straight fence from $A$ to a point $D$ on $[BC]$.

Find $BD$. Fully justify any assumptions you make.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $BC$ using the cosine rule: $BC^2 = 110^2 + 85^2 - 2 \times 110 \times 85 \times \cos 55^\circ$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>
- State the value of $BC$: $BC = 92.7 \text{ m}$ ($92.7314\dots \text{ m}$) <marks>A1</marks>

**METHOD 1**
- Justify that since the height and area of triangles $ABD$ and $ADC$ are equal, their bases must be equal <marks>R1</marks>
- State that $D$ is the midpoint of $[BC]$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate the value of $BD$: $BD = 46.4 \text{ m}$ ($46.365\dots \text{ m}$) <marks>A1</marks>

**METHOD 2**
- Let $C\hat{B}A = \theta$
- Calculate the value of $\theta$ using the sine rule: $\frac{\sin \theta}{110} = \frac{\sin 55^\circ}{92.731\dots}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the value of $\theta$: $\theta = 76.3^\circ$ ($76.3354\dots^\circ$)
- Substitute values into the area formula for triangle $ABD$: $\frac{1}{2} \times 85 \times BD \times \sin(76.33\dots^\circ) = \frac{3829.53\dots}{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate the length of $BD$: $BD = 46.4 \text{ m}$ ($46.365\dots \text{ m}$) <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>In Method 1, the final two marks are dependent on the **R1** being awarded.</note>

steps.title: Identify the side length BC
steps.content:
To solve for the segment $BD$, we first need to find the total length of the side $BC$ of the triangular field. Since we know two sides and the included angle, we can use the **Cosine Rule**.

From the **Geometry and Trigonometry** section of your data booklet, the formula is:

$$c^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}-2ab	ext{cos } C$$

steps.title: Calculate length BC
steps.content:
Let $a = 110$, $b = 85$, and the angle $C = 55^\circ$. Substituting these values into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} BC^2 &= 110^2 + 85^2 - 2(110)(85)\cos 55^\circ \\ BC^2 &= 12100 + 7225 - 18700 \times \cos 55^\circ \\ BC &= \sqrt{19325 - 18700 \times \cos 55^\circ} \end{aligned}$$

Using your calculator, we find:
$$BC \approx 92.7314... \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the formula directly into the run-matrix menu. Ensure the angle mode is set to degrees.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x^2] for the square root. Type 110^2 + 85^2 - 2 * 110 * 85 * cos(55). Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the calculator. Click the wrench icon to ensure it is in 'Degrees' mode.

calculator.description: Calculate the side length BC using the cosine rule.

highlights.text: angle between these two sides is 55^\circ

steps.title: Justify that D is midpoint
steps.content:
The farmer wants to "divide the field into two equal parts." This means the area of triangle $ABD$ must equal the area of triangle $ADC$.

Both triangles share the same vertex $A$, so they share the same perpendicular height $h$ from $A$ to the line $BC$. Using the area formula for a triangle, $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$:

$$\text{Area}_{ABD} = \frac{1}{2} \times BD \times h$$
$$\text{Area}_{ADC} = \frac{1}{2} \times DC \times h$$

Since the areas and heights are equal, their bases must also be equal: $BD = DC$. Therefore, $D$ is the midpoint of $[BC]$.

highlights.text: divide the field into two equal parts

steps.title: Solve for BD
steps.content:
Because $D$ is the midpoint of the line segment $[BC]$, the distance $BD$ is simply half of the total length $BC$:

$$\begin{aligned} BD &= \frac{1}{2} \times BC \\ BD &= \frac{92.7314...}{2} \\ BD &\approx 46.365... \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get:
$$BD = 46.4 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Type 92.73144364 / 2 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Type 92.73144364 / 2 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 92.73144364 / 2 into the calculator.

steps.title: Calculate length BC
steps.content:
To begin, we need the length of the third side of the field, $[BC]$. Since we know two sides and the included angle ($55^\circ$), we apply the **Cosine Rule** from the data booklet.

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

Substituting our known values:
$$BC^2 = 110^2 + 85^2 - 2(110)(85) \cos(55^\circ)$$

$$BC = \sqrt{12100 + 7225 - 18700 \cos(55^\circ)}$$
$$BC \approx 92.7314... \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression in a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the side length BC.

highlights.text: angle between these two sides is 55°

steps.title: Calculate the total area
steps.content:
Next, we find the total area of the field $ABC$. This is required because the farmer wants to divide the field into two equal parts. We use the **Area formula** for a triangle:

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab \sin C$$

$$\text{Area}_{ABC} = \frac{1}{2} \times 85 \times 110 \times \sin(55^\circ)$$
$$\text{Area}_{ABC} \approx 3829.54 \text{ m}^2$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula in a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the total area of triangle ABC.

steps.title: Determine the angle B
steps.content:
To solve for $BD$ in the triangle $ABD$ later, we need to know the measure of angle $B$ (also written as $C\hat{B}A$). We can find this using the **Sine Rule**:

$$\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B}$$

$$\frac{92.7314...}{\sin(55^\circ)} = \frac{110}{\sin B}$$

Rearranging to solve for $B$:
$$\sin B = \frac{110 \times \sin(55^\circ)}{92.7314...}$$
$$B = \arcsin(0.97169...)$$
$$B \approx 76.335...^\circ$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the inverse sine of the expression.

ti84.instructions: Use the arcsin function. Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the inverse sine in a new cell.

calculator.description: Solve for angle B (in degrees).

steps.title: Calculate the length BD
steps.content:
The farmer divides the field into two equal parts, so we assume the area of triangle $ABD$ is exactly half of the total area:

$$\text{Area}_{ABD} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{Area}_{ABC} = \frac{3829.54...}{2} \approx 1914.77 \text{ m}^2$$

Now we use the area formula specifically for triangle $ABD$ with side $AB = 85$ and angle $B$:

$$\text{Area}_{ABD} = \frac{1}{2} \times AB \times BD \times \sin B$$

$$1914.77 = \frac{1}{2} \times 85 \times BD \times \sin(76.335...^\circ)$$

Solving for $BD$:
$$BD = \frac{1914.77}{42.5 \times \sin(76.335...^\circ)}$$
$$BD \approx 46.4 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the division expression.

ti84.instructions: Perform the division on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression in a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the length BD.

highlights.text: divide the field into two equal parts

steps.title: Final Justification
steps.content:
We assumed that "two equal parts" refers to the area of the triangles. Since $\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle ABC$ share the same height relative to the base $[BC]$, having half the area implies that $BD$ is half the length of $BC$. 

Verification:
$$BD = \frac{1}{2} \times 92.7314... \approx 46.4 \text{ m}$$

The answer is $46.4 \text{ m}$ (to 3 significant figures).

parts.content:
Consider a unit vector $\mathbf{u}$, such that $\mathbf{u} = p\mathbf{i} - \frac{2}{3}\mathbf{j} + \frac{1}{3}\mathbf{k}$, where $p > 0$.

Point $C$ is such that $\vec{BC} = 9\mathbf{u}$.

Find the coordinates of $C$.
parts.markscheme:
• Attempt to find coordinates of $C$ M1
    e.g. $\vec{BC} = \begin{pmatrix} 9p \\ -6 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$, $C = 9\mathbf{u} + B$, $\vec{BC} = \begin{pmatrix} x-9 \\ y-5 \\ z+6 \end{pmatrix}$
  • Correct working to find expression for $C$ A1
    e.g. $\vec{OC} = \begin{pmatrix} 9p+9 \\ -1 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$, $C = 9 \begin{pmatrix} p \\ -2/3 \\ 1/3 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 9 \\ 5 \\ -6 \end{pmatrix}$, $y=-1$ and $z=-3$
  • Correct approach to find $|\mathbf{u}|$ (seen anywhere) A1
    e.g. $p^2 + (-\frac{2}{3})^2 + (\frac{1}{3})^2$, $\sqrt{p^2 + \frac{4}{9} + \frac{1}{9}}$
  • Recognizing unit vector has magnitude of 1 M1
    e.g. $|\mathbf{u}|=1$, $\sqrt{p^2 + (-\frac{2}{3})^2 + (\frac{1}{3})^2}=1$, $p^2 + \frac{5}{9} = 1$
  • Correct working to solve for $p$ A1
    e.g. $p^2 = \frac{4}{9}$, $p = \pm \frac{2}{3}$
  • $p = \frac{2}{3}$ A1
  • Attempt to substitute their value of $p$ into expression for $C$ M1
    e.g. $\begin{pmatrix} x-9 \\ y-5 \\ z+6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ -6 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$, $C = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ -6 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 9 \\ 5 \\ -6 \end{pmatrix}$, $x-9=6$
  • $C(15, -1, -3)$ (accept $\begin{pmatrix} 15 \\ -1 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$) A1

8 marks total

The marks for finding $p$ are independent of the first two marks. For example, it is possible to award marks such as (M0)(A0)A1(M1)(A1)A1 (M0)A0.

parts.content: Find the gradient of the line DC.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute coordinates into gradient formula: $\frac{1 - (-2)}{-3 - 1}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the gradient: $-\frac{3}{4}$ $(-0.75)$ <marks>A1 FT</marks>

<note>Award <marks>M1</marks> for correct substitution of their Part 1 answer into gradient formula. Follow through from Part 1.</note>

steps.title: Identify the coordinates
steps.content:
To find the gradient of the line segment $DC$, we first need the coordinates of both points. From the question, we have point $D$:

$$D(-3, 1)$$

Based on the markscheme for Part 1, the coordinates for point $C$ are:

$$C(1, -2)$$ 

This question relates to **Topic 2: Functions** in your syllabus, specifically finding the gradient between two points.

steps.title: Select the gradient formula
steps.content: In your IB formula booklet, under the **Functions** section, you can find the formula for the gradient ($m$) of a line passing through two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$.

steps.title: Substitute the values
steps.content:
Now, let's substitute the coordinates of $D(-3, 1)$ and $C(1, -2)$ into the formula. Let's set $D$ as $(x_2, y_2)$ and $C$ as $(x_1, y_1)$:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \frac{1 - (-2)}{-3 - 1} \\ m &= \frac{1 + 2}{-4} \end{aligned}$$

Notice how the double negative in the numerator becomes a positive! This is a common spot for small errors, so watch out for those signs.

steps.title: Calculate the final gradient
steps.content:
Finally, simplify the fraction to find the gradient of $DC$:

$$m = -\frac{3}{4}$$

You can also express this as a decimal, $-0.75$. Both forms are usually acceptable in IB exams unless a specific format is requested.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the numerator and denominator separately or use the fraction template (ALPHA + Y=).

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into an empty cell to see the decimal result.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient using the substitution shown.

steps.title: Identify the coordinates
steps.content:
To find the gradient of the line $DC$, we first need the coordinates of both points. From the question, we have point $D$, and from our work in Part 1, we have point $C$:

*   $D(-3, 1)$
*   $C(1, -2)$

steps.title: Input coordinates into GDC
steps.content:
Since we are using the **GDC Linear Regression** approach, we can input these two points into the statistics tool of your calculator. 

We will enter the $x$-coordinates into one list (often $L_1$) and the $y$-coordinates into a second list (often $L_2$):

*   $x$-values: $\{-3, 1\}$
*   $y$-values: $\{1, -2\}$

gdc.instructions:
1. Open the Statistics or Spreadsheet app.
2. Enter the x-coordinates (-3, 1) in the first column and y-coordinates (1, -2) in the second column.
3. Select Menu -> Statistics -> Stat Calculations -> Linear Regression (mx+b).
4. Select your columns and click OK.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] and select 1:Edit.
2. Enter -3 and 1 into L1.
3. Enter 1 and -2 into L2.
4. Press [STAT], arrow over to CALC, and select 4:LinReg(ax+b).
5. Ensure Xlist is L1 and Ylist is L2, then select Calculate.

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the '+' icon and select 'Table'.
2. Enter the points (-3, 1) and (1, -2) into the table.
3. In a new cell, type y1 ~ mx1 + b.

calculator.description: Perform a linear regression to find the slope (gradient).

steps.title: Identify the gradient
steps.content:
After running the linear regression, your calculator will provide the value for the slope (usually labeled as $a$ or $m$). This value represents the gradient of the line.

From the GDC output, we find:
$$m = -0.75$$

You can also express this as a fraction: 
$$m = -\dfrac{3}{4}$$

This matches the concept of the gradient formula found in your **Data Booklet**:

$$\text{Gradient: } m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

steps.title: Identify coordinates
steps.content:
To find the gradient using simultaneous equations, we first need the coordinates of both points. From the question, we have point $D(-3, 1)$. Based on the previous part of this problem (Part 1), the coordinates for point $C$ are $(1, -2)$.

We will substitute these into the general equation for a straight line:

$$y = mx + c$$

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the $(x, y)$ values of points $D$ and $C$ into the equation $y = mx + c$ to create a system of two equations.

For point $D(-3, 1)$:
$$1 = -3m + c \quad \text{--- (Equation 1)}$$

For point $C(1, -2)$:
$$-2 = 1m + c \quad \text{--- (Equation 2)}$$

steps.title: Solve for the gradient
steps.content:
To find the gradient $m$, we can eliminate $c$ by subtracting Equation 2 from Equation 1:

$$\begin{aligned} 1 - (-2) &= (-3m + c) - (m + c) \\ 1 + 2 &= -3m - m + c - c \\ 3 &= -4m \end{aligned}$$

Now, divide both sides by $-4$:

$$m = -\dfrac{3}{4}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Equation/Solver menu.
2. Select 'Simultaneous' and 2 unknowns.
3. Enter the coefficients from: -3m + 1c = 1 and 1m + 1c = -2.
4. Press SOLVE.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [MATRX], go to EDIT, and select [A] as a 2x3 matrix.
2. Enter the coefficients: [[-3, 1, 1], [1, 1, -2]].
3. Press [2nd] [QUIT], then [2nd] [MATRX] -> MATH -> rref([A]).
4. The first value in the bottom right will be your gradient m.

desmos.instructions:
Create a list of the points and use the regression tool to find m.
1. Type (-3, 1) and (1, -2) into a table.
2. In a new row, type y1 ~ mx1 + c.
3. Desmos will provide the value for m.

calculator.description: You can verify the gradient by solving the system of linear equations on your calculator.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The gradient $m$ of the line $DC$ is:

$$m = -\dfrac{3}{4}$$

(or $-0.75$).

In the IB markscheme, you earn **M1** for the correct substitution of coordinates and **A1** for providing the final correct gradient.

parts.content: Find the equation of the line DC. Write your answer in the form $ax + by + d = 0$ where $a$, $b$ and $d$ are integers.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute gradient and point into line equation: $y - 1 = -\frac{3}{4}(x + 3)$ **OR** $y + 2 = -\frac{3}{4}(x - 1)$ **OR** $y = -\frac{3}{4}x - \frac{5}{4}$ <marks>M1</marks>

**OR**

- Substitute into $y = mx + c$: $1 = -\frac{3}{4}(-3) + c$ **OR** $-2 = -\frac{3}{4}(1) + c$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State final equation in integer form: $3x + 4y + 5 = 0$ <marks>A1 FT</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept any integer multiple, including negative multiples. Award <marks>M1</marks> for correct substitution of their Part 2 answer and a given point. Follow through from Parts 1 and 2. Where the gradient in Part 2 is found to be $\frac{5}{0}$ (undefined), award at most <marks>M1 A0</marks> for either $x = -3$ or $x + 3 = 0$.</note>

steps.title: Identify point and gradient
steps.content:
To find the equation of line $DC$, we need a point on the line and the line's gradient. From the question, we are given the coordinates for point $D$. 

From the previous part of the question (Part 2), we found the gradient of the line to be:
$$m = -\frac{3}{4}$$

steps.title: Substitute into point-gradient formula
steps.content:
Now, we use the point-gradient formula:

$$(y - y_1) = m(x - x_1)$$

This is a standard method in **Topic 2: Functions** for finding a line's equation when you know one point $(x_1, y_1)$ and the gradient $m$.

Substitute $x_1 = -3$, $y_1 = 1$, and $m = -\dfrac{3}{4}$:

$$y - 1 = -\dfrac{3}{4}(x - (-3))$$

Simplifying the double negative:
$$y - 1 = -\dfrac{3}{4}(x + 3)$$

steps.title: Rearrange into integer form
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer in the form $ax + by + d = 0$ where $a, b,$ and $d$ are integers. 

First, multiply the entire equation by $4$ to clear the fraction:
$$4(y - 1) = -3(x + 3)$$

Next, expand the brackets:
$$4y - 4 = -3x - 9$$

Finally, move all terms to the left side of the equation:
$$\begin{aligned} 3x + 4y - 4 + 9 &= 0 \\ 3x + 4y + 5 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type 3*(-3) + 4*(1) + 5 into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Go to the home screen and type 3*(-3) + 4*(1) + 5. If the result is 0, your equation is correct.

desmos.instructions: Enter the equation 3x + 4y + 5 = 0 and then plot the point (-3, 1) to see if it lies on the line.

calculator.description: You can verify that point D fits the equation by substituting its coordinates into your final answer.

highlights.text: ax + by + d = 0 where a, b and d are integers

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The equation of the line $DC$ is:
$$3x + 4y + 5 = 0$$

steps.title: Identify point and gradient
steps.content:
To find the equation of the line DC, we need its gradient ($m$) and a point on the line. From the previous part of the question, we know the gradient of line DC is $m = -\frac{3}{4}$. We are given that point D has the coordinates $(-3, 1)$.

- Gradient ($m$): $-\frac{3}{4}$
- Point D: $x = -3, y = 1$

steps.title: Solve for the y-intercept
steps.content:
We use the slope-intercept form equation $y = mx + c$. By substituting our known gradient and the coordinates of point D, we can solve for the y-intercept ($c$).

$$\begin{aligned} y &= mx + c \\ 1 &= -\dfrac{3}{4}(-3) + c \end{aligned}$$

Now, let's calculate $c$:

$$\begin{aligned} 1 &= \dfrac{9}{4} + c \\ c &= 1 - \dfrac{9}{4} \\ c &= -\dfrac{5}{4} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type 1 - 9/4 in the main calculation screen. Use the fraction conversion tool if necessary.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 - (9 / 4) and press ENTER. To see the result as a fraction, press MATH, select 1:>Frac, and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type c = 1 - 9/4 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of c by subtracting 9/4 from 1.

steps.title: Write the initial equation
steps.content:
Now that we have both the gradient ($m = -\frac{3}{4}$) and the y-intercept ($c = -\frac{5}{4}$), we can write the equation of the line in slope-intercept form:

$$y = -\frac{3}{4}x - \frac{5}{4}$$

steps.title: Convert to general form
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer in the form $ax + by + d = 0$, where $a, b,$ and $d$ are integers. 

First, multiply the entire equation by the common denominator, $4$, to remove the fractions:

$$4y = -3x - 5$$

Finally, rearrange the terms to one side of the equation to set it equal to zero:

$$3x + 4y + 5 = 0$$

highlights.text: Write your answer in the form $ax + by + d = 0$ where $a$, $b$ and $d$ are integers.

steps.title: Verify the final answer
steps.content:
We can verify our result by checking if the coordinates of point D $(-3, 1)$ satisfy the equation $3x + 4y + 5 = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} 3(-3) + 4(1) + 5 &= -9 + 4 + 5 \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Since the equation holds true, we have found the correct line equation. This step would earn the final **A1** mark in an exam.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate 3(-3) + 4(1) + 5 on the home screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3*(-3) + 4*(1) + 5 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 3(-3) + 4(1) + 5 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Verify the point (-3, 1) in the equation 3x + 4y + 5 = 0.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Right triangle formed by ladder, wall, and ground; given hypotenuse (10 ft) and angle with ground (60°), so use SOHCAHTOA to find adjacent distance and opposite height.

parts.content: Calculate the distance from the base of the ladder to the wall.
parts.markscheme:
- Let $x$ be the distance from the wall to the base of the ladder
- Identify the horizontal distance: $x = 10\cos(60^\circ)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the value: $x = 5$ feet <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award full marks for correct answer with no working shown</note>

steps.title: Visualize the right triangle
steps.content:
To solve this, we can imagine the ladder, the wall, and the ground forming a right-angled triangle:

*   The **hypotenuse** is the length of the ladder: $10$ feet.
*   The **angle** between the ladder and the ground is $60^\circ$.
*   The **adjacent side** is the horizontal distance along the ground from the ladder's base to the wall ($x$).

This setup falls under **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry** in your syllabus, specifically using right-angled trigonometry (SOH CAH TOA).

highlights.text: makes a 60^\circ angle with the ground

steps.title: Identify the cosine ratio
steps.content:
Since we know the hypotenuse and want to find the side **adjacent** to the $60^\circ$ angle, we use the **Cosine** ratio from the SOH **CAH** TOA mnemonic:

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}$$

In our case:
*   $\theta = 60^\circ$
*   $\text{Adjacent} = x$
*   $\text{Hypotenuse} = 10$

steps.title: Solve for the distance
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our values into the formula and solve for $x$:

$$\cos(60^\circ) = \frac{x}{10}$$

To isolate $x$, multiply both sides by $10$:

$$x = 10 \times \cos(60^\circ)$$

In the IB markscheme, setting up this equation correctly earns you the first method mark (**M1**).

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
Since $\cos(60^\circ) = 0.5$, the calculation is straightforward:

$$x = 10 \times 0.5 = 5$$

The distance from the base of the ladder to the wall is **$5$ feet**. This final value earns the accuracy mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Visualize the triangle
steps.content:
To solve this, we first visualize the ladder leaning against the wall. This forms a right-angled triangle where:
- The ladder is the **hypotenuse** ($10$ feet).
- The wall is vertical ($90^\circ$ to the ground).
- The angle between the ladder and the ground is $60^\circ$.

This falls under **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry** in your IB syllabus.

steps.title: Calculate the top angle
steps.content:
In any triangle, the sum of the interior angles is always $180^\circ$. We know two angles: the right angle at the base of the wall ($90^\circ$) and the angle at the ground ($60^\circ$). 

Let's find the angle at the top of the ladder (between the ladder and the wall):

$$\text{Top Angle} = 180^\circ - 90^\circ - 60^\circ = 30^\circ$$

steps.title: Apply the Sine Rule
steps.content:
Now we use the **Sine Rule** from your formula booklet. This rule relates the sides of a triangle to the sines of their opposite angles.

$$\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{c}{\sin C}$$

In our case:
- Side $a$ is the distance from the base to the wall (which we want to find).
- Angle $A$ is the opposite angle at the top ($30^\circ$).
- Side $c$ is the ladder ($10$ feet).
- Angle $C$ is the opposite right angle ($90^\circ$).

steps.title: Solve for the distance
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the formula and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{x}{\sin(30^\circ)} &= \frac{10}{\sin(90^\circ)} \\ x &= 10 \times \frac{\sin(30^\circ)}{\sin(90^\circ)} \end{aligned}$$

Since $\sin(90^\circ) = 1$ and $\sin(30^\circ) = 0.5$, we get:

$$x = 10 \times 0.5 = 5 \text{ feet}$$

steps.title: Visualize the triangle
steps.content:
A ladder leaning against a wall forms a right-angled triangle. 

*   The **ladder** is the hypotenuse ($10$ feet).
*   The **angle with the ground** is $60^\circ$.
*   The **wall and ground** meet at a $90^\circ$ angle.

This setup is covered in **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry (SL 3.2)**. Understanding how to label your triangle is the first step toward solving any trig problem!

highlights.text: makes a $60^\circ$ angle with the ground

steps.title: Identify the special angles
steps.content:
In any triangle, the sum of the interior angles is $180^\circ$. We can find the missing angle at the top of the ladder:

$$\text{Top Angle} = 180^\circ - 90^\circ - 60^\circ = 30^\circ$$

Because the angles are $30^\circ$, $60^\circ$, and $90^\circ$, we have a **special 30-60-90 triangle**.

steps.title: Apply the geometric property
steps.content:
For a 30-60-90 triangle, there is a very helpful shortcut: 

**The side opposite the $30^\circ$ angle is exactly half the length of the hypotenuse.**

Looking at our triangle, the distance from the base of the ladder to the wall is the side **opposite** the $30^\circ$ angle. This means we can find the distance without using a calculator for complex trig functions!

steps.title: Calculate the final distance
steps.content:
Now, we just apply the property to our known hypotenuse length ($10$ feet):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Distance} &= \frac{1}{2} \times \text{Hypotenuse} \\ \text{Distance} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \\ \text{Distance} &= 5 \text{ feet} \end{aligned}$$

In an IB exam, showing this logic or the equivalent trig expression ($10 \cos 60^\circ$) earns you the method mark (**M1**), and the correct value with units earns the accuracy mark (**A1**).

1 mark total

parts.content: Find the vector product $\overrightarrow{PQ} \times \overrightarrow{PR}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to calculate the vector (cross) product <marks>M1</marks>
- $\overrightarrow{PQ} \times \overrightarrow{PR} = \begin{pmatrix} (1)(1) - (3)(4) \\ (3)(1) - (2)(1) \\ (2)(4) - (1)(1) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -11 \\ 1 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Do not award M1 if less than 2 entries are correct.</note>

parts.content: Hence, find a unit vector that is perpendicular to the stabilization platform $PQR$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to calculate the magnitude of the vector product found in part (c) <marks>M1</marks>
- $|\overrightarrow{PQ} \times \overrightarrow{PR}| = \sqrt{(-11)^2 + 1^2 + 7^2} = \sqrt{171}$ ($3\sqrt{19} \approx 13.076...$)
- Unit vector $= \frac{1}{\sqrt{171}}\begin{pmatrix} -11 \\ 1 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}$ (or $\begin{pmatrix} -0.841 \\ 0.0765 \\ 0.535 \end{pmatrix}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept the negative of this vector.</note>

questions.specification:
A design for a modern architectural museum includes a large triangular shade sail. The corners of the sail are attached to three points, $A$, $B$, and $C$, relative to a 3D coordinate system where the ground is represented by the $xy$-plane. The attachment points are defined by the coordinates $A(0, 0, 10)$, $B(12, 0, 0)$, and $C(0, 16, 0)$, where all coordinates are measured in meters. Point $O(0, 0, 0)$ is the origin, located on the ground directly below point $A$.

parts.content: Find the total surface area of the triangular shade sail $ABC$.
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** the lengths of the remaining edges $AB$ and $AC$: $AB = \sqrt{244} \approx 15.62$ and $AC = \sqrt{356} \approx 18.87$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Attempt** to use the cosine rule to find an internal angle of $\triangle ABC$, for example $B\hat{A}C$: $20^{2} = 244 + 356 - 2(\sqrt{244})(\sqrt{356})\cos A$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the angle: $A = 70.2^{\circ} (70.1601 \ldots^{\circ})$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Attempt** to use the area of a triangle formula: $\frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{244} \times \sqrt{356} \times \sin(70.1601 \ldots^{\circ})$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the area: $139 \text{ m}^{2} (138.636 \ldots)$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total
